Tafseer:
A question asked out of haste:
The various meanings of 'su'al' (asking/questioning) have been discussed in their relevant places. Here, its connection with the particle 'bi' is a proof that it implies haste (isti'jal) or mockery (istihza). That is, a questioner demanded the prompt arrival of the impending punishment or mocked it. When the rebellious leaders of the Quraysh were warned of the punishment, they would ask: Where is that punishment? If the punishment is to come, why does it not arrive? How long have we been hearing these warnings; if it departed, where on the path did it anchor that it has not reached us yet? Such questions, obviously, were not asked for research, but to exasperate the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and to mock the punishment. In these questions, the meanings of both haste and mockery are found; rather, one can say that the purpose of this haste was solely to mock. The connection with the particle 'bi' has clarified this implicit meaning of 'su'al', which is why both haste and mockery are connected with 'bi'—for example: (They ask you to hasten the punishment) (Al-Ankabut, 29:54). Similarly, 'istihza' (mockery) is also connected with 'bi'.

The foolishness of those who hasten the punishment:
'For the disbelievers' (lil-kafireen) can be linked to 'impending' (waqi'), meaning: they mock the punishment that is impending for the disbelievers. It can also be regarded as an independent sentence—namely: the punishment they mock will be specifically for the disbelievers, and no one will be able to prevent it. The meaning is: it is their own misfortune that they are hastening it with such audacity. If that flood of destruction arrives, whose house will it devastate? It will devastate their own houses! What preparation have they made against it that they are challenging it in this manner! No one will have the power to prevent it—neither they themselves nor their assumed deities; so on what basis are they inviting it!

Do not compare the Days of Allah with your days: That is, the punishment is bound to come. Every promise and warning of the God Who threatened it is bound to happen, but His presence is highly elevated. He is the owner of great heights, ascents, and stairways. The commands issued from His presence are in accordance with His own plan, based on the years and days that are recognized with Him. A single Day with Him is equivalent to a thousand years of human calculation. For this reason, people should not measure His days and years with their own limited scales. It is this narrow-mindedness of man that makes him impatient and hasty regarding God's decisions. He tries to hasten things in every matter, forgetting that Allah Almighty has created different worlds, each with its own orbit and system. We can neither know all the secrets of His universe, nor calculate His days with our days and hours. In Surah Al-Hajj, reference is made to this reality in these words:

(And they urge you to hasten the punishment. But Allah will never fail in His promise. And indeed, a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of those which you count.) (Al-Hajj, 22:47)

The meaning is: your scales are very small. You measure every promise and warning according to your own days; therefore, you feel that a long time has passed since a promise was made and it has not been fulfilled, whereas according to the divine calculation, not more than a second or a minute has passed.

The meaning of Allah being 'Owner of the Ways of Ascent': This explains Allah being 'Dhi-l-Ma'arij' (Owner of the Ways of Ascent)—showing that even for the angels and Gabriel to reach His elevated presence, it takes a Day equivalent to fifty thousand years. 'Ma'arij' means stairways and ascents. That is, Allah is beyond all limits and heights. To reach Him—let alone others—even the angels and Gabriel require a Day equivalent to fifty thousand years to cover the stages of this path.
These matters belong to the category of 'mutashabihat' (allegorical verses), the actual reality of which is impossible for us to comprehend. They are intended solely to convey the concept: do not compare God's matters with your own. A Day with Him is equivalent to a thousand of your years, and for certain tasks, He has also appointed Days equivalent to fifty thousand years. The verse under discussion points to this specific Day.
'The Spirit' (Ar-Rooh) refers to Gabriel. Mentioning him specifically after the general mention of angels is due to his high status, as he is the leader of all angels. This word is used in the Qur'an for Gabriel.
Clearing a misunderstanding: Some have interpreted this Day as the Day of Resurrection, regarding its length as an expression of its terror and severity. In our view, this opinion is contrary to the context as well as Arabic grammar. Here, the objective is to express the height of Allah's presence, not the severity of the Day of Resurrection. The severity and terror of the Day of Resurrection are described in the subsequent verses.
It should be noted here that although God's presence is highly elevated and He is beyond all creation, He is at the same time closer to everyone than their jugular vein. He sees everyone, hears everyone, and supervises everyone. Although we are unable to see Him, He sees us at all times. Our sights cannot perceive Him, but He perceives our sights: (Sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives sights) (Al-An'am, 6:103). For this reason, it is neither permissible for anyone to be secure from Him, nor to despair of Him.
The same is the case with the angels. Although they require a Day equivalent to fifty thousand years to ascend to Him, all angels are nevertheless in His sight at all times. He can command them whenever He wills, and seize them whenever He wills.

Instruction of beautiful patience to the Prophet:
This describes the attitude the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) should adopt against those who hasten the punishment. He is commanded: practice patience with beauty and dignity against their haste and mockery. 'With beauty' means: neither become disheartened or disappointed by their attitude, nor take any hasty step in response, and do not allow any weakness to creep into your stance. Along with the instruction of patience in various situations, indications are also given to the matters that are necessary to make patience a 'beautiful patience'.

People of insight do not consider the Resurrection distant:
'Indeed, they see it as distant, but We see it as near' (innahum yarawnahu ba'eeda wa narahu qareeba) - That is, their sight is very limited; therefore, they regard the punishment of which they are warned as very distant, whereas We see it as very near, and the true seeing is Our seeing. If they possessed insight, they too would see it as near, but their eyes are veiled; therefore, a near thing appears distant to them. When that Day comes, this veil will be removed, and everyone will see that what they regarded as very distant was actually very near.
(But today your sight is sharp) (Qaf, 50:22)
points to this reality. It should noted that those who possess insight alongside sight do not fall into this delusion. A person of insight sees with the light of Allah; therefore, just as Allah Almighty sees that Day as near, the believer also sees it as near.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

Depiction of the terror of the Resurrection:
This portrays the upheaval and terror of the Resurrection so that those who do not see it with the eyes of insight may take lesson from this depiction. It is stated: on that Day, the heaven will become like dregs of oil, and the mountains like carded wool.
The word 'muhl' has various meanings. This word appeared in Surah Al-Kahf, and we explained its meaning in the appropriate place. It is used in the sense of oil and dregs of oil. Taking this meaning, the objective is to compare the color of the heaven to the dark, red dregs of oil. The redness of the heaven is also mentioned in Surah Ar-Rahman:
(And when the heaven is split open and becomes rose-colored like oil) (Ar-Rahman, 55:37).

'And the mountains will be like wool' (wa takunu-l-jibalu ka-l-'ihn) - The word 'ihn' means wool, and here it refers to carded wool, as stated in Surah Al-Qari'ah:
(And the mountains will be like carded wool) (Al-Qari'ah, 101:5).
These matters belong to the conditions of the Resurrection, which are from the allegorical verses. Their actual reality cannot be comprehended here. However, the mind inclines to the view that the redness of the heaven will be a result of the raging flames of Hell, and the mountains will be dispersed like carded wool so that those who regarded the mountains as permanent may witness their transience. Obviously, when the mountains face this end, what of other things! These matters are narrated to warn those foolish individuals who boasted of their power, population, fortresses, and strongholds. This shows that on that Day, the entire system will be devastated, and a new world will appear with completely new laws.

Depiction of distress on that Day:
That is, on that Day, everyone will be in such a state of distress that close friends—who were devoted to one another in the world, sacrificing their lives and wealth—will turn their eyes away, such that no one will even ask about another's state.

That is, they will not be hidden from one another so that their feelings of support and defense might not arise; rather, they will be made to see each other, and this entire misery will pass before their eyes, yet the moment will be of such distress that no one will ask about another's state. This theme is also described in Surah 'Abasa, verse 34, where its full explanation will appear, if Allah wills.
'The criminal will wish that he could be ransomed from the punishment of that Day by his children, and his wife and his brother, and his nearest kindred who sheltered him, and whoever is on earth entirely, then it would save him' (yawaddu-l-mujrimu law yaftadi min 'adhabi yawma'idhim-bi-baneeh, wa sahibatihi wa akheeh, wa faseelatihi-l-lati tu'weeh, wa man fi-l-ardi jamee'an thumma yunjeeh) - The punishment of that Day will be so terrifying that the criminals will wish to ransom themselves from it by giving their most beloved relatives. A father will wish to ransom his children, a husband his wife, a brother his brother, a member of a family his family—indeed, he will wish to ransom the entire creation of the earth to save his own life. But this wish will remain nothing but a wish. On that Day, no one will have anything to offer as ransom, nor will any ransom be accepted; rather, everyone must face the punishment they deserve.
If you reflect, you will see that all the relationships for which a person possesses natural love, defense, and support are mentioned here. Alongside the mention of family, the specific attribute 'who sheltered him' (tu'weeh) is added—meaning the family that served as his refuge and shelter throughout his life, protecting him from enemies and for whose defense he remained ready to sacrifice his life, he will wish to ransom even them to save himself.
It should be noted that in nomadic life, protecting and defending the family and tribe was of great importance. One who stood firm in defending the tribe was regarded as a hero, while one who compromised the tribe's interests or shrank from its defense in times of danger could neither show his face anywhere nor could his descendants.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11, 12, 13, and 14 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11, 12, 13, and 14 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11, 12, 13, and 14 was combined in the original text.]

On that Day, there will be no escape for anyone:
That is, this wish to escape the punishment will never be fulfilled. Its flames will peel away the skin of the criminal from a distance.
'No!' (Kalla) refutes the wish of the criminals mentioned above. That is, their wish to escape the punishment will never be fulfilled.
'Indeed, it is a flame' (innaha ladha) - The reference of the pronoun 'innaha' is the punishment mentioned above. Since it refers to the punishment of the Fire, the pronoun is feminine. 'Ladha' means a flame. 'Shawa' refers to the skin of the head and body. That is, the heat of the flames of this Fire will be such that it will peel away the skin of the criminals from a distance.
'Nazzi'atan' can be regarded as a second predicate (khabar) after 'ladha', or as a circumstantial clause (hal). In both cases, there is no significant difference in meaning.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 15 and 16 was combined in the original text.]

That is, today when they are invited to worship Allah, protect themselves from His punishment, and spend in His way, they turn their backs and avert; but on that Day, there will be no possibility of turning away from the punishment of Hell. Hell will call back all those who turned away and denied. The word 'tad'u' (calls/invites) is highly eloquent here. That is: those whose hearts are not affected by the loving call of the Prophet should remember that one day Hell will call these hard-hearted individuals, and it will call them in such a manner that no escape will remain for anyone.

The end of the misers:
This points specifically to the misers, as the call to believe in the Hereafter and to spend in charity is most difficult for their hearts. The success of the Hereafter requires a person to deposit his wealth in the bank of God instead of locking it up in the safes of this world. The miser regards this as a losing transaction. He neither believes in recompense, nor possesses the courage to sacrifice his cash wealth for the promise of the Hereafter. Thus, for this reason, misers have been foremost in opposing the call of the prophets. Miserliness and denying the recompense are interconnected. In the Qur'an, He states:
(Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan, and does not feel the urge to feed the needy) (Al-Muzzammil, 107:1-3).
Using 'fa-aw'a' (and hoarded) alongside 'jama'a' (collected) is intended to show that they accumulated wealth from every source, both lawful and unlawful, with closed eyes, and then kept it secured with great care. In another place, this characteristic of the misers is described by: (who collects wealth and counts it) (Al-Humazah, 104:2). It is stated: such misers, who turned away from the Prophet's call to spend because they did not believe in recompense, will be called back by the Fire of Hell on that Day.

'Haloo' means hasty, impatient, and of small heart.
Using a general word to point to a specific group: Although the word 'insan' (human) is general, here it points specifically to those humans whose mention has appeared above—showing that when Allah granted them respite, instead of appreciating it, they hastened the punishment, and as soon as they are seized, they will begin to wail. God gave them wealth, but instead of using it to earn for the Hereafter, they sat on it like dragons, regarding it as the fruit of their own capability, intelligence, and desert, and assuming that this wealth is a proof of their being approved by God, so it will remain with them forever, and if there is a Hereafter, they will receive even more there. But as soon as any adversity befalls them, they immediately lose hope and begin to wail. The meaning is: do not care about the boasting and pride of such people. They are highly superficial and small-hearted individuals.
When a specific group is mentioned through a general word in this manner, the objective—as we have explained in its relevant place—is to show indifference and even contempt. It is as if the speaker does not consider them worthy of being addressed or pointed to directly. This style is well known in our language as well.
Imbalance among the inner inclinations: 'Indeed, mankind was created impatient' (inna-l-insana khuliqa haloo'a) is a style similar to: (Man was created of haste) (Al-Anbiya, 21:37) or (And man is ever hasty) (Al-Isra, 17:11). Just as lust, anger, greed, and other such inclinations have their place in human nature, haste also has its place. This quality is not blameworthy in itself; what is blameworthy is its display in an inappropriate place or in an imbalanced manner. Allah Almighty created human beings with conflicting inclinations and desired of them to establish balance and harmony among them in accordance with His commands taught through the prophets, subjecting them to His pleasure and laws. Success in the Hereafter depends entirely on passing this test. The honor of choice (ikhtiyar) is also granted to him for this test. If this test were not intended, there would be no meaning in honoring him with choice, nor any reason for him to possess superiority over other creations.
In the verse, the quality described as 'hala'' is also a form of haste and impatience. If this quality dominates a person to the extent that other conflicting inclinations are subdued by it, then under such circumstances, it is entirely accurate to say that he is created of this element. If salt in a pot of food increases to an intolerable degree, one can say that this food is prepared of salt.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19, 20, and 21 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19, 20, and 21 was combined in the original text.]

The group free from imbalance:
This refers to those who are free from this imbalance or deviation described above. It is stated: those who pray are excepted from this.
This shows that in establishing proper balance and moderation among conflicting inclinations of the soul, the primary role belongs to prayer. Whoever wishes to train themselves so that Satan does not lead them into deviation must first prioritize the commitment to prayer. Without this, no one can succeed in this training.
Prayer establishes proper balance: As for the question of what is within prayer that gives it this primary role in training, this is not the place for a detailed answer. We have written a treatise on this titled 'The Reality of Prayer'. Those who seek details may refer to it. Here, we will address only those aspects to which the Qur'an has pointed in this place.

Consistency in action produces spirit:
'Those who are constant in their prayer' (alladhina hum 'ala salatihim da'imun) - It is stated: for this training, only the prayer of those who are constant in it is beneficial. This is a highly important warning. The Qur'an has mentioned in various ways those who otherwise never perform remembrance or prostration, but as soon as a calamity befalls them, they become great performers of prayer and make long supplications. Then, as soon as that phase passes, they turn away as if they never had any concern with God, nor will they have in the future. Since such prayer is merely a manifestation of the very imbalance whose remedy is being described here, it is of no benefit to their training. The beneficial prayer is the one performed constantly, maintaining its commitment in both good and difficult circumstances. A small action performed consistently and continuously is far more blessed than a large action performed occasionally. A single heavy downpour, if followed by a long drought, causes crops to dry up and perish; conversely, even a light rain, if continuous, keeps fields lush and productive. The same is the case with prayer. If a Muslim fulfills the general religious demands consistently, the blessing in his action will be far greater than those who occasionally become great performers of prayer but then forget it to the extent of not even looking at the mosque. Aisha compared the Prophet's practice to a continuous rain—meaning whatever action he performed, he did so consistently, even if it was small. This consistency of action generates power, spirit, and life within it.

After prayer, spending is the second pillar of religion:
This describes the second pillar of religion—namely, spending in the way of Allah. We have clarified in various places that among religious deeds, primary importance belongs to prayer and spending. Prayer connects the servant with God, while spending establishes his relationship with his fellow human beings on a correct foundation.
'A known right' (haqqum-ma'lum) does not necessarily imply that it refers to zakat. The system of zakat with a specific rate was implemented in the Madinese period, whereas this Surah is Meccan; rather, it refers to the reality that their spending is not merely a symbolic compliance to get rid of a duty, but they give a portion of their wealth in a known amount as an obligatory right in the cause of God.
In the pre-Islamic period of Jahiliyyah, people were not unfamiliar with zakat and spending. Surah Al-An'am indicates that the polytheists also set aside a portion of their wealth as God's right, but under their polytheistic beliefs, they associated their idols in it. The followers of Haneefiyyah (monotheists) were sincere monotheists; therefore, they set aside this portion solely as Allah's right. The narration regarding Prophet Ishmael (peace be upon him) was preserved among them:
(And he used to command his people to prayer and zakat) (Maryam, 19:55).
Therefore, it is incorrect to assume that before Islam, the Arabs were completely unfamiliar with any specific form of spending in the way of Allah. The rate of zakat fixed in the Madinese period could be different from what was previously common among the people, but it cannot be denied that a specific form of zakat was well known among the righteous of the pre-Islamic period. Indeed, for this reason, the word zakat has appeared repeatedly in Meccan Surahs. Like other teachings of the Abrahamic faith, this zakat was also lost behind the veils of innovations, but its name remained. Islam highlighted it and implemented it in its perfect form within the Islamic society.

The beggar and the deprived:
'For the beggar and the deprived' (li-s-sa'ili wa-l-mahroom) - 'Sa'il' refers to the one who begs of people. His very action bears witness that he is needy; therefore, he deserves help. Regarding such beggars, there is no need for deep investigation. One should give what one can, and if unable, make a polite excuse. Rebuking or insulting them is not permissible, as prohibited in the Qur'an and Hadith. Here, the attribute of the true believers is described: their wealth contains a known right for the beggars.
'Mahroom' refers to the one who is deprived of the means of livelihood, but since this word appears here alongside 'sa'il', it also implies that despite being deprived, he does not stoop to the level of begging of people. Some needy individuals are so self-respecting that they face hunger but refuse to suffer the disgrace of begging. Particularly, those who were once wealthy but fell into need due to a turn of fortune are the primary category of 'mahroom'. They must be helped by searching them out, rather than waiting for them to ask. Their self-respect does not allow them to beg.

The primary motive of spending:
This points to the motive of their spending: since they believe in the Day of Recompense, they have set aside a known right in their wealth for the poor and needy. We noted above that for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, recognizing the rights of the poor and needy is highly difficult, as stated:
(Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan) (Al-Ma'un, 107:1-2).

'And those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord' (wa-l-adhina hum-min 'adhabi rabbihim-mushfiqun) - This explains their belief in the Day of Recompense: because they believe in recompense, they remain constantly fearful of their Lord's punishment. This inner state of theirs is described in Surah Ad-Dahr as:
(They fulfill their vows and fear a Day whose evil will be widespread. And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive, [saying], 'We feed you only for the countenance of Allah. We wish not from you reward or gratitude. Indeed, We fear from our Lord a harsh, distressful Day.') (Ad-Dahr, 76:7-10).
This motive of spending is the natural, sincere, disinterested, and pure motive; therefore, the Qur'an has highlighted it. Other motives besides this are commercial motives, which neither reform the soul nor are accepted with God. This point has been clarified previously and will be further discussed.

This is a highly eloquent qualification of the preceding point: those who fear their Lord's punishment in this manner are highly wise. The punishment of their Lord is something against which no one should feel secure even for a moment. No one knows when it might strike, and when it does, there is no one who can prevent it or even divert its direction. This shows that the most effective way to remain secure from Allah's punishment is to feed and clothe the poor and needy.

Purity of character:
Following prayer, spending, and fear of God, this points to the purity of their character: they guard their private parts. They do not behave like unbridled beasts, breaking all limits of Allah. They restrict their sexual desires to their wives and slave-girls (footnote 1). They do not dare to transgress this limit. And those who transgress this limit should remember that they are violators of Allah's limits—meaning they will face the punishment for violating His limits, as God will surely take retribution from those who disrespect His limits.
_____
Footnote 1: The issue of slave-girls and slaves has been discussed in detail in the commentary of Surah An-Noor.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 29, 30, and 31 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 29, 30, and 31 was combined in the original text.]

A single comprehensive quality that unites all virtues:
A single comprehensive quality unites many virtues: 'And those who are keepers of their trusts and their covenant.'
The word 'amanah' (trust) is not used in a narrow sense, as commonly assumed; rather, all the powers, capabilities, resources, and means with which Allah Almighty has blessed a person are in his custody as a trust, and He will question him about each of them one day to see if any betrayal (khiyanah) was committed. Indeed, the Qur'an states that the faculties of hearing, sight, and intellect will be questioned, and whoever commits betrayal will face punishment. Similarly, it is stated that every blessing will be questioned on the Day of Resurrection.
Similarly, the word 'ahd' (covenant) is used in a wide sense here. It includes the covenant established among ourselves through mutual agreements, the covenant that is not established through formal agreements but is recognized and respected in every good society, the covenant Allah has taken from our nature, and most importantly, the covenant and pledge Allah took from people through His prophets, the clauses of which are clearly documented in the Shariah.

Testimony in a wide sense:
Just as the words 'trust' and 'covenant' are used in a wide sense in the preceding verse, the word 'testimony' (shahadah) is used in a wide sense in this verse. Everyone is responsible for performing the testimony in the smallest of matters for which they have undertaken responsibility, as well as the great testimony (Al-Shahadat-ul-Kubra) to which Allah has appointed them in the verse:
(That you may be witnesses over mankind and the Messenger may be a witness over you) (Al-Baqarah, 2:143).

Prayer is the fortress of religion:
Finally, reference is made again to prayer. In verses 22-23, the description of these qualities began with prayer, and now this section concludes with it, which clearly shows that prayer is both the source and the protector of all these virtues. It is as if prayer is the fortress of all religion and morality. Whoever protects this fortress protects his religion, and whoever breaks this fortress destroys his entire religion. Caliph Umar clarified this reality, saying: 'Whoever destroys his prayer will destroy the rest of his religion to an even greater degree.'
Note also that while the earlier verse mentioned constancy in prayer, this final verse emphasizes guarding it. 'Guarding' (muhafadhah) means protecting prayer from all dangers and flaws that can destroy its benefit—such as performing it without observing its times, failing to attend the congregation, or committing actions that are contrary to its objectives. We have explained these dangers and flaws in our treatise 'The Reality of Prayer' as well as in our book 'Purification of the Soul'; refer to them for details (footnote 1).
_____
Footnote 1: With minor variations in wording, these verses have appeared at the beginning of Surah Al-Mu'minun, where we discussed every part in detail.

It is stated: those who possess the aforementioned qualities will be in gardens, honored and respected.
Warning to the arrogant Quraysh: This contains a rebuke to those arrogant leaders of the Quraysh who, due to their hereditary leadership, assumed they were approved by God. Their assumption was: just as they are entitled to blessings in this world, they will be entitled to high ranks in the Hereafter, while those who are poor here will remain poor there. They are informed that Paradise is not inherited, but depends on a person's character and deeds. Those who adopt the character approved by Allah will enter Paradise, whether they are rich or poor; and those who lack this character will not even smell its fragrance, whether they are from the leaders of the Arabs or the non-Arabs.

Upon hearing this decision of the Qur'an, the arrogant Quraysh would burn with rage. How could they tolerate hearing that Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his poor companions would reside in Paradise, while the leaders of the Quraysh, the inheritors of all honor, would become fuel for Hell? In this rage, they would gather in groups (footnote 1), rushing toward the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) from his right and left to deny and mock him (footnote 2). These verses depict this situation and express amazement at the foolishness of these arrogant individuals. It is asked: What is the matter with those who disbelieved, hastening before you, in groups? Does every person among them expect to be admitted into a garden of pleasure? That is: if they are in this vain hope, their expectation will never be fulfilled.
This vain hope of these wealthy individuals is mentioned in various places in the Qur'an.
In Ha-Meem As-Sajdah, it is stated:
(And I do not think the Hour will occur. And even if I should be returned to my Lord, indeed, for me with Him is the best.) (41:50).
In Surah Al-Qalam, they are refuted as follows:
(Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is the matter with you? How do you judge? Or do you have a scripture in which you study, [proving] that you will indeed have therein whatever you choose?) (Al-Qalam, 68:35-38).
_____
Footnote 1: The Qur'an uses the word 'izeen', which is the plural of 'izah', meaning groups or bands.
Footnote 2: The word 'ihtia' means to hasten or rush toward something.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 36, 37, and 38 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 36, 37, and 38 was combined in the original text.]

This deals a blow to their vain assumption: they should not boast of their sanctity and greatness. Their dream will never be realized. We have created them from what they know; let them not forget it—namely: water, clay, and dust. It is not fitting for a creature created from such elements to assume themselves inherited possessors of Paradise based on hereditary pride! No one possesses honor based solely on creation; honor and desert are achieved only through the deeds approved by Allah. If a person lacks this treasure, then from the perspective of creation, not only all humans but even the most impure animals are equal to him. This theme appeared in Surah An-Najm as:
(He is most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him.) (An-Najm, 53:32).
We pointed out in the commentary of Surah An-Najm that this verse also deals a blow to the pantheistic beliefs of some.

A specific style of Arabic grammar:
This is an oath similar to the one in the previous Surah, verses 38-39.
The particle 'la' appearing before the oath was explained in the previous Surah.
'The easts' (mashariq) and 'the wests' (magharib) have been discussed. In the Qur'an, these words are used in singular, dual, and plural forms, and in all three cases, there is no significant difference in meaning. In Arabic, the dual is sometimes used to point to the two extremes of something, as in 'between the two cliffs' in Surah Al-Kahf. Similarly, the plural is sometimes used to show the vastness of the corners of something, as explained under Surah Al-A'raf.
Here, Allah Almighty has sworn by His self as the Lord of all the easts and wests to prove His limitless power—showing that He can resurrect people after they have decayed whenever He wills, and no obstacle can stand in the way of His decree. The element of testimony, which is the primary objective of an oath, is entirely clear. In this universe, we witness daily that the sun, moon, and billions of stars rise and set by the command of the Lord of the universe; so, is it difficult for the All-Powerful and Eternal God—Who possesses the authority to make them disappear daily and then reappear—to resurrect people after they have turned to dust? Is the first task more difficult or the second? He Who is powerful over the first, why should He be unable to perform the second!
In Surah An-Naziat, this proof is expressed in a more comprehensive style:
(Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it. He raised its ceiling and proportioned it. And He darkened its night and extracted its bright day. And the earth after that He spread. He extracted from it its water and its pasture, and the mountains He firmly fixed.) (An-Naziat, 79:27-32).

The matter upon which the oath was sworn:
'To substitute [for them] better than them; and We are not to be outrun' (ala an nubaddila khayram-minhum wa ma nahnu bi-masbooqeen) - That is: We are powerful over substituting for them a better creation who will believe in Allah's religion. Another meaning can be: We are powerful over recreating them in a better form. The meaning is: since We are powerful over creating them in a better form, resurrecting them as they are is even easier for Us. Generally, commentators have preferred the first meaning, and from the perspective of language, there is no flaw in it; however, my inclination is toward the second meaning. This is because the primary subject of the Surah is the occurrence of the Resurrection, which the deniers regarded as impossible after they had decayed. The subsequent verses support this view.

Finally, this is comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and a threat for the deniers: if they do not understand, leave them. Let them remain occupied in their vain talk and play until they face the Day they are threatened of, which they are ignoring.

The word 'nusub' is the plural of 'naseeb', which means a stone firmly fixed in the ground. It can refer to the stones fixed by the polytheists to present their offerings and sacrifices, or the stones fixed as goalposts or markers for races.
Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and Dahhak interpreted it as a marker fixed for a race.
Abul Aliyah and Yahya bin Kathir interpreted it as a goalpost or marker toward which a race is run.
'Iphad' means 'isra'—namely, to run or hasten toward something.
In my view, 'nusub' here means a marker or goalpost. The meaning is: today these rebels behave arrogantly against Allah's caller and deviate from His path to follow other paths, but the Day is coming when the caller of the Resurrection will blow his Trumpet, and they will emerge from their graves and hasten toward him as if they were running toward the markers of a race. The meaning is: on that Day, all their arrogance will vanish, and their deviation will end. The manner in which people will hasten at the call of the caller is described in the Qur'an as:
(On that Day, they will follow the Caller, [in whom] there is no deviousness) (Ta-Ha, 20:108).
That is, just as an arrow goes straight to its target, they will hasten toward the caller. Generally, commentators have interpreted 'nusub' as the altars or idols of the polytheists, but this is a secondary meaning, and running toward altars carries no meaningful concept. No such practice among the polytheists is known to me.

That is, today when Allah's Messenger warns them of that Day, they rush to attack him; but when the caller of the Resurrection calls them, they will hasten toward him with their eyes humbled in fear, covered in humiliation. It is stated: this is the Day they had been threatened of. Recall here the question asked in the first verse: (A questioner asked of an impending punishment). After discussing its essential aspects and portraying its depiction, this final verse answers: this is that Day of punishment of which they were warned but which they mocked. Thus, the entire discourse is connected from beginning to end.
